section.title: The Temple Completed and Dedicated
section.sectionRationale: Darius searches the archives, finds Cyrus's original decree, and not only confirms it but orders the very enemies of the Jews to fund and protect the work. The temple is finished, dedicated with joy, and the returned exiles keep the Passover, purified and separated to seek the LORD. It is the climax of the first half of Ezra: the house of God completed against all opposition, entirely by the hand of God.
section.thesis: God overrules the empire and its officials to fund, protect, and complete His house, so that opposition ends in provision and the dedication and Passover are kept with a joy that testifies the LORD Himself turned the heart of the king and finished the work.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Darius's decree confirming and funding the work, verses 1 to 12; the temple finished, verses 13 to 15; the joyful dedication, verses 16 to 18; and the Passover and Feast of Unleavened Bread kept with joy, verses 19 to 22.

units.label: The decree of Darius
units.note: leave this work alone; pay its full cost

verses.text: Thus King Darius ordered a search of the archives stored in the treasury of Babylon.
And a scroll was found in the fortress of Ecbatana, in the province of Media, with the following written on it: Memorandum:

In the first year of King Cyrus, he issued a decree concerning the house of God in Jerusalem: Let the house be rebuilt as a place for offering sacrifices, and let its foundations be firmly laid. It is to be sixty cubits high and sixty cubits wide,
with three layers of cut stones and one of timbers. The costs are to be paid from the royal treasury.

Furthermore, the gold and silver articles of the house of God, which Nebuchadnezzar took from the temple in Jerusalem and carried to Babylon, must also be returned to the temple in Jerusalem and deposited in the house of God.
Therefore Darius decreed: To Tattenai governor of the region west of the Euphrates, Shethar-bozenai, and your associates and officials in the region: You must stay away from that place!
Leave this work on the house of God alone. Let the governor and elders of the Jews rebuild this house of God on its original site.

I hereby decree what you must do for these elders of the Jews who are rebuilding this house of God: The cost is to be paid in full to these men from the royal treasury out of the taxes of the provinces west of the Euphrates, so that the work will not be hindered.

Whatever is needed, young bulls, rams, and lambs for burnt offerings to the God of heaven, as well as wheat, salt, wine, and oil, as requested by the priests in Jerusalem, must be given to them daily without fail.
Then they will be able to offer sacrifices of a sweet aroma to the God of heaven and to pray for the lives of the king and his sons.

I also decree that if any man interferes with this directive, a beam is to be torn from his house and raised up, and he is to be impaled on it. And his own house shall be made a pile of rubble for this offense.

May God, who has caused His {{shem|Name}} to dwell there, overthrow any king or people who lifts a hand to alter this decree or to destroy this house of God in Jerusalem. I, Darius, have issued the decree. Let it be carried out with diligence.

units.label: The temple finished
units.note: completed in the sixth year of Darius

verses.text: In response, Tattenai the governor of the region west of the Euphrates, Shethar-bozenai, and their associates diligently carried out what King Darius had decreed.

So the Jewish elders built and prospered through the prophesying of Haggai the prophet and Zechariah son of Iddo. They finished building according to the command of the God of Israel and the decrees of Cyrus, Darius, and Artaxerxes, kings of Persia.
And this temple was completed on the third day of the month of Adar, in the sixth year of the reign of King Darius.

units.label: The joyful dedication
units.note: a sin offering for all Israel

verses.text: Then the people of Israel, the priests, the Levites, and the rest of the exiles, celebrated the {{chanukkah|dedication}} of the house of God with joy.
For the dedication of the house of God they offered a hundred bulls, two hundred rams, four hundred lambs, and a sin offering for all Israel of twelve male goats, one for each tribe of Israel.
They also appointed the priests by their divisions and the Levites by their groups to the service of God in Jerusalem, according to what is written in the Book of Moses.

units.label: The Passover kept with joy
units.note: the LORD had made them joyful

verses.text: On the fourteenth day of the first month, the exiles kept the {{pesach|Passover}}.
All the priests and Levites had purified themselves and were ceremonially clean. And the Levites slaughtered the Passover lamb for all the exiles, for their priestly brothers, and for themselves.
The Israelites who had returned from exile ate it, together with all who had {{badal|separated}} themselves from the uncleanness of the peoples of the land to seek the LORD, the God of Israel.

For seven days they kept the Feast of Unleavened Bread with joy, because the LORD had made them joyful and turned the heart of the king of Assyria toward them to strengthen their hands in the work on the house of the God of Israel.

shem.gloss: Name. Darius's decree speaks of the God who has caused His Name to dwell in Jerusalem, echoing the Old Testament theology of the temple as the place of God's Name, His revealed presence and ownership. Even a pagan king acknowledges that this house belongs to the God whose Name is there.

chanukkah.gloss: Dedication, the setting apart of the completed house for God's service; the same root later gives its name to the Feast of Dedication, Hanukkah. Here the word marks the joyful consecration of the finished temple, a house begun in tears now given wholly to the LORD.

pesach.gloss: Passover, the feast commemorating Israel's redemption from Egypt when the destroyer passed over the blood-marked homes. Kept here by the restored community, from the Hebrew section that resumes at 6:19, it frames the whole return as a new exodus and points to Christ, our Passover Lamb, 1 Corinthians 5:7.

badal.gloss: To separate, divide, set apart. Those who ate the Passover were the returned exiles together with all who had separated themselves from the uncleanness of the surrounding peoples to seek the LORD. The word marks holiness as consecration to God, and it graciously includes outsiders who turned to seek Him.

basic.context: King Darius orders a search and finds Cyrus's original decree authorizing the temple. He confirms it and goes further: he commands Tattenai and the officials to stop opposing the work, to pay its full cost from the royal treasury, and even to supply the animals and provisions for the sacrifices. The temple is finished in Darius's sixth year, and the people dedicate it with joy, offering a sin offering for all twelve tribes. Then they keep the Passover, purified and separated to seek the LORD, full of joy because God had turned the king's heart. The question is: who finished this house? The LORD did, by ruling kings and reversing His enemies' plans.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the triumphant conclusion of the first half of the book, told as history with a strong theme of God's sovereignty. The great reversal, enemies forced to fund the temple, is meant to make us marvel at God's providence, not to promise that opposition always ends this way. The Passover and dedication are fulfilled in Christ, so we celebrate their meaning rather than repeating their rituals.
basic.originalLanguages: Even Darius confesses the God who caused His Name, His shem, to dwell in Jerusalem. The finished house is set apart in joyful chanukkah, dedication. Then the people keep the pesach, the Passover, and eat it with all who had badal, separated themselves, from uncleanness to seek the LORD.
basic.typology: The completed temple points to Christ, the true dwelling of God with man. The Passover lamb points to Christ, our Passover, sacrificed for us. And the sin offering for all Israel points to the one sacrifice that covers the whole people of God.
basic.christ: The temple is finished, and its whole purpose, God dwelling with His people, is fulfilled in Jesus, in whom all the fullness of God dwells and who is the true meeting place of God and man. The Passover the exiles kept looks straight to Christ, our Passover Lamb, whose blood causes judgment to pass over us. And the joy of the dedication foreshadows the joy of the finished work of salvation.
basic.god: God is gloriously sovereign. He turns the heart of the king, reverses the schemes of enemies so that they must pay for the very house they opposed, and completes what He began. He is the God whose Name dwells among His people, and who makes them joyful.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 5:7 names Christ as our Passover, sacrificed for us. Proverbs 21:1 says God turns the king's heart wherever He wills. And John 2:19-21 shows Christ as the true temple.
basic.oneStory: The first half of Ezra ends in triumph: the house of God is finished, dedicated with joy, and crowned with the Passover, all because God ruled kings and reversed His enemies. It points to the greater house and the greater Passover in Christ, and to the final joy of God dwelling with His people forever.

context.literary: The chapter resolves the tension of chapters 4 and 5. Darius's decree answers the enemies' letter with a stunning reversal, and the narrative then races to the completion, dedication, and Passover, each marked by joy. At verse 19 the text returns from Aramaic to Hebrew, fittingly, as the story moves from imperial documents back to the covenant life of Israel keeping its feasts.
context.historical: Persian kings did keep meticulous archives, so the recovery of Cyrus's memorandum at Ecbatana is entirely plausible. The temple was completed in 516 BC, about seventy years after its destruction in 586 BC, a striking match to Jeremiah's seventy years. Royal patronage of local temples, including funding sacrifices and requesting prayers for the king, fits documented Persian policy toward subject peoples.
context.canonical: This completion fulfills the hope of chapter 3 and the preaching of Haggai and Zechariah. The dedication and Passover tie the restored community to the exodus and to Solomon's temple dedication, while the sin offering for all twelve tribes keeps alive the vision of one whole people of God, a vision consummated in Christ, the true temple and Passover Lamb, in whom Jew and Gentile become one.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that God completed His house by sovereignly ruling the Persian kings and reversing the enemies' opposition into provision, and to celebrate the joy of a restored people worshiping God in the dedicated temple and the kept Passover.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a unique historical triumph and the keeping of Old Testament feasts fulfilled in Christ; it does not prescribe that God's work will always be government-funded or opposition-free. What endures is God's sovereign faithfulness to finish His work, the call to holy separation to seek the LORD, and the joy of worship centered on the sacrifice that Passover foreshadows.

debates.question: Why did God allow years of opposition, only for it to end in the empire funding the temple?

views.label: God turns opposition into greater blessing
views.case: The reversal, enemies compelled to pay for the house they fought, displays God's sovereign power and shames the opposition; the delay set the stage for a triumph no one could credit to human strength, magnifying the LORD who rules kings' hearts.

views.label: God's timing trains and completes
views.case: The pause coincided with the ministry of Haggai and Zechariah and taught the people dependence; God finished the work in His own appointed time, so that the completion answered His word rather than mere circumstance.

debates.bearing: Both readings exalt God's providence: behind a frowning providence He hid a smiling face, weaving even fierce opposition into the completion and funding of His house.

debates.question: Why offer a sin offering of twelve goats for all Israel when only some tribes had returned?

views.label: Representing the whole covenant people
views.case: The twelve goats confess that Israel is one people of twelve tribes before God, even when scattered; the returned remnant offers atonement on behalf of the entire nation, refusing to write off the exiles who had not come home.

views.label: Hope for a full restoration
views.case: The number twelve voices an eschatological hope that God will yet gather all His people; the offering looks beyond the small remnant to the complete Israel God intends to save, a hope realized in Christ.

debates.bearing: Either way, the offering testifies to one undivided people of God, a unity finally secured in Christ, who gathers all His own, Jew and Gentile, into one body.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's key words trace the movement from imperial decree to covenant joy. Even Darius acknowledges the God who caused His shem, His Name, to dwell in Jerusalem, conceding that the temple belongs to the God whose presence and ownership rest upon it. When the house is finished, the people keep its chanukkah, its dedication, joyfully setting the building apart for God, the same root that would later name the Feast of Dedication. Then, as the text shifts back into Hebrew, the community keeps the pesach, the Passover, reliving the exodus and framing their whole return as a new redemption. And they eat it with all who had badal, separated themselves from the uncleanness of the peoples to seek the LORD, showing that holiness is consecration to God, and that the door stands open even to outsiders who turn to Him. Name, dedication, Passover, separation: the vocabulary of a house and a people wholly given to God.

typology.type: The completed temple where God's Name dwells
typology.fulfillment: Christ, in whom all the fullness of the Deity dwells and who is the true temple, Colossians 2:9 and John 2:19-21.

typology.type: The Passover lamb kept by the exiles
typology.fulfillment: Christ our Passover, sacrificed for us, whose blood turns judgment away, 1 Corinthians 5:7 and John 1:29.

typology.type: The sin offering for all twelve tribes
typology.fulfillment: The one sacrifice of Christ that atones for the whole people of God, gathered from every tribe, Hebrews 10:12-14 and Revelation 7:9.

study.christ: The finished temple realized its whole reason for being, God dwelling among His people, and that purpose comes to its fullness in Jesus, in whom the fullness of God dwells bodily and who is Himself the true temple. The Passover the restored exiles kept with joy points unmistakably to Christ, our Passover Lamb, sacrificed for us, whose blood causes God's judgment to pass over all who shelter under it. And the sin offering for all twelve tribes foreshadows the single, sufficient sacrifice by which Christ atones for the whole people of God, drawn from every tribe and nation. The joy of this dedication is a foretaste of the joy of the finished work of the cross and of the day God will dwell with His people forever.
study.god: God is sovereign over kings and reverses the plans of His enemies with ease, turning the heart of Darius so that the very officials who opposed the work are made to fund and protect it. He is faithful to complete what He begins, finishing the house in His appointed time, close to the seventy years Jeremiah foretold. He is the God whose Name dwells among His people, holy and near, and He is the giver of joy, for the text says the LORD had made them joyful. This is a God who finishes His work and fills His people with gladness.

crossReferences.note: Christ our Passover has been sacrificed; therefore let us keep the feast, the fulfillment of the exiles' Passover.
The king's heart is a stream in the LORD's hand, the truth behind Darius's decree and the turned heart of verse 22.
Jesus speaks of the temple of His body, the true dwelling of God that this house foreshadowed.
God's promise that the glory of the latter house would exceed the former, pointing beyond stone to Christ.

study.oneStory: The first half of Ezra closes in triumph and joy: God rules the empire, reverses His enemies, and finishes His house, which His people dedicate and crown with the Passover. Every part points forward, the temple to Christ the true dwelling of God, the Passover to Christ our Lamb, the sin offering for all Israel to the one sacrifice that gathers the whole people of God. The house begun in tears is finished in joy, a sure sign that the God who starts His work will surely complete it in Christ.

quiz.q: What did Darius do after Cyrus's decree was found in the archives?

options.text: He confirmed it and ordered the officials to fund the work fully and stop opposing it.
options.note: Verses 6-12 record Darius confirming the decree and commanding full support.

options.text: He cancelled the project as too expensive.
options.note: The opposite: he ordered it funded from the royal treasury.

options.text: He ignored the decree and did nothing.
options.note: He acted decisively to confirm and enforce it.

options.text: He ordered the temple destroyed.
options.note: He protected the temple and threatened those who would harm it.

quiz.q: What is remarkable about who ended up paying for the temple?

options.text: The enemies who had opposed the work were commanded to fund and supply it.
options.note: God reversed the opposition so the officials paid for the very house they resisted.

options.text: The Jews alone paid for everything themselves.
options.note: The people gave, but Darius ordered the royal treasury to pay the full cost.

options.text: No money was needed at all.
options.note: Real funding was required, and Darius decreed it be paid in full.

options.text: A foreign army seized the funds by force.
options.note: The funds came by royal decree, not by force.

quiz.q: How did the people respond when the temple was completed?

options.text: They dedicated the house of God with joy and kept the Passover.
options.note: Verses 16-22 describe the joyful dedication and the Passover.

options.text: They mourned that it was not as grand as Solomon's.
options.note: That was the earlier reaction at the foundation; here the note is joy.

options.text: They immediately abandoned it and returned to Babylon.
options.note: They stayed, worshiped, and celebrated with joy.

options.text: They argued over who should be high priest.
options.note: The chapter records joyful, ordered worship, not dispute.

quiz.q: Who does the text say was ultimately behind the temple's completion?

options.text: The LORD, who turned the king's heart and made His people joyful.
options.note: Verse 22 credits the LORD with turning the king's heart to strengthen their hands.

options.text: The skill of the Jewish builders alone.
options.note: Human effort was real, but the text credits God's sovereign hand.

options.text: The generosity of Tattenai the governor.
options.note: Tattenai obeyed Darius; the LORD is named as the true cause.

options.text: Good luck and favorable timing.
options.note: The text names God's providence, not luck.

quiz.q: How does the exiles' Passover point to Christ?

options.text: The Passover lamb foreshadows Christ, our Passover, sacrificed so that judgment passes over us.
options.note: 1 Corinthians 5:7 makes Christ the fulfillment of the Passover.

options.text: It teaches that we must keep the Passover ritual to be saved.
options.note: The feast is fulfilled in Christ; we celebrate its meaning, not repeat the ritual for salvation.

options.text: It shows the exiles no longer needed sacrifice.
options.note: They kept the Passover sacrifice, which pointed forward to Christ's.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ.
options.note: The Passover points directly to Christ, our Passover Lamb.

questions: God turned the plans of the enemies into provision for His house. Where do you need to trust that God can reverse opposition and even make it serve His purposes?
The people dedicated the finished house with joy after years of delay and tears. How does remembering that God completes what He begins encourage you in an unfinished, discouraging work?

Those who ate the Passover had separated themselves from uncleanness to seek the LORD, and outsiders who turned to God were welcomed too. What might holy separation to seek God, without proud exclusion, look like for you?
The whole point of the temple was God dwelling with His people, fulfilled in Christ. How does knowing that God dwells with you in Jesus shape your daily life?
The text says the LORD had made them joyful. Where is your joy resting on circumstances rather than on the God who finishes His work, and how might that change?

translationNotes.note: The dimensions sixty cubits high and sixty cubits wide are unusual; the BSB footnote notes a Syriac reading closer to Solomon's temple, sixty long, twenty wide, thirty high. The figures come from the Persian memorandum.
The temple was completed in the sixth year of Darius, 516 BC, close to seventy years after its destruction, matching Jeremiah's prophecy of seventy years.
From this verse the text returns to Hebrew after the long Aramaic section that began at 4:8; the shift accompanies the move from imperial documents back to Israel's covenant worship.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Ezra 6:1-22. Em dashes in verses 9 and 16 were rendered as commas per house style. Words drawn from 6:1-18 are marked Aramaic and from 6:19-22 Hebrew. No em or en dashes remain.
